name: All-or-Nothing Thinking
description: Seeing things in black-and-white categories. If performance falls short of perfect, you see yourself as a total failure.
examples: I didn't finish everything on my to-do list, so the whole day was a waste.
If I can't do it perfectly, there's no point in trying at all.
I made one mistake in my presentation, so it was a complete disaster.
reframingTips: Look for the gray area between the two extremes.
Ask yourself: Is there a middle ground between total success and total failure?
Replace 'always' and 'never' with 'sometimes' and 'occasionally'.
Acknowledge partial successes and progress made.

name: Overgeneralization
description: Viewing a single negative event as a never-ending pattern of defeat by using words like 'always' or 'never'.
examples: I got rejected from that job, so I'll never get hired anywhere.
My coworker criticized my report - nobody ever appreciates my work.
This project failed, so everything I do fails.
reframingTips: Look for evidence that contradicts the generalization.
Replace 'always/never' with specific, time-limited language.
Remind yourself of past successes in similar situations.
Consider whether you're applying one experience to all situations.

name: Mental Filtering
description: Picking out a single negative detail and dwelling on it exclusively, so that your vision of all reality becomes darkened.
examples: I got great feedback on my review except for one area, which means I'm failing.
The whole vacation was ruined because of that one rainy day.
I focus only on the one critical comment and ignore the ten positive ones.
reframingTips: Deliberately notice and list positive aspects of the situation.
Ask yourself: Am I focusing on just one part of the picture?
Practice gratitude by noting three good things each day.
Consider whether you would judge a friend this harshly.

name: Catastrophizing
description: Expecting the worst-case scenario to happen, or blowing things out of proportion. Also called magnification.
examples: I made an error in my email - I'm going to get fired for sure.
This headache must mean something is seriously wrong with me.
If I speak up in the meeting, everyone will think I'm incompetent.
reframingTips: Ask yourself: What's the most likely outcome, not the worst?
Consider: Has this worst-case scenario ever actually happened?
Rate the actual probability of the catastrophic outcome (0-100%).
Think about how you've handled similar situations in the past.

name: Mind Reading
description: Assuming you know what other people are thinking without any evidence, usually believing they are thinking negatively about you.
examples: My boss didn't say hello this morning - she must be upset with me.
They're probably thinking I'm not qualified for this role.
He looked at me funny - he must think my idea was stupid.
reframingTips: Remind yourself that you cannot read minds.
Consider alternative explanations for their behavior.
Ask directly instead of assuming what others think.
Focus on observable facts rather than interpretations.

name: Fortune Telling
description: Predicting that things will turn out badly, as if you have a crystal ball. Treating predictions as established facts.
examples: I just know the presentation will go horribly.
There's no point in applying - I won't get the job.
This new initiative is going to fail, I can feel it.
reframingTips: Remind yourself that you cannot predict the future.
Look for evidence both for and against your prediction.
Consider times when your negative predictions were wrong.
Focus on what you can control rather than predicting outcomes.

name: Emotional Reasoning
description: Assuming that your negative emotions necessarily reflect the way things really are: 'I feel it, therefore it must be true.'
examples: I feel overwhelmed, so my situation must be hopeless.
I feel incompetent, therefore I must be incompetent.
I feel guilty, so I must have done something wrong.
reframingTips: Recognize that feelings are not facts.
Ask yourself: What evidence supports this feeling?
Consider whether you would feel differently after rest or a good day.
Separate the emotion from the reality of the situation.

name: Should Statements
description: Using 'should', 'must', or 'ought to' statements that set rigid rules about how you or others must behave, leading to guilt and frustration.
examples: I should be able to handle this without feeling stressed.
I must always be productive at work.
They should have known better than to say that.
reframingTips: Replace 'should' with 'I would prefer' or 'It would be nice if'.
Ask yourself: Is this rule realistic and flexible?
Consider whether you're holding yourself to an impossible standard.
Practice self-compassion when you don't meet your own rules.

name: Labeling
description: Attaching a negative label to yourself or others instead of describing the specific behavior. An extreme form of overgeneralization.
examples: I'm a loser because I didn't get the promotion.
He's a jerk because he disagreed with me.
I'm lazy because I took a break.
reframingTips: Describe the specific behavior instead of using a global label.
Recognize that one action doesn't define your entire identity.
Ask yourself: Would I label a friend this way for the same behavior?
Use specific, behavioral language instead of character judgments.

name: Personalization
description: Seeing yourself as the cause of some negative external event that you were not primarily responsible for.
examples: My team didn't meet the deadline because of me.
My child is struggling in school - I must be a bad parent.
The project failed because I wasn't good enough.
reframingTips: Consider all the factors that contributed to the outcome.
Ask yourself: What percentage of responsibility is actually mine?
Recognize that many outcomes are influenced by multiple people and factors.
Distinguish between influence and control.
